content:
# Leviticus Chapter 23 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71v–72r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_023_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_023_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 109–110 | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: The appointed times of the LORD that you shall proclaim as holy convocations — these are my appointed times.

**3** Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day is a sabbath of solemn rest, a holy convocation. You shall do no work. It is a sabbath to the LORD in all your dwelling places.

**4** "These are the appointed times of the LORD, the holy convocations, which you shall proclaim at the time appointed for them.

**5** In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at twilight, is the LORD's Passover.

**6** And on the fifteenth day of the same month is the Feast of Unleavened Bread to the LORD; for seven days you shall eat unleavened bread.

**7** On the first day you shall have a holy convocation; you shall do no ordinary work.

**8** But you shall present a food offering to the LORD for seven days. On the seventh day is a holy convocation; you shall do no ordinary work."

**9** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**10** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land that I give you and you reap its harvest, you shall bring the sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest to the priest,

**11** and he shall wave the sheaf before the LORD, so that you may be accepted. On the day after the sabbath the priest shall wave it.

**12** And on the day when you wave the sheaf, you shall offer a male lamb a year old without blemish as a burnt offering to the LORD.

**13** And the grain offering with it shall be two tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, a food offering to the LORD with a pleasing aroma, and the drink offering with it shall be of wine, a fourth of a hin.

**14** And you shall eat neither bread nor roasted grain nor fresh grain until this same day, until you have brought the offering of your God: it is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwelling places.

**15** "You shall count from the day after the sabbath, from the day that you brought the sheaf of the wave offering. Seven complete weeks shall there be.

**16** You shall count fifty days to the day after the seventh week. Then you shall present a grain offering of new grain to the LORD.

**17** You shall bring from your dwelling places two loaves of bread to be waved, made of two tenths of an ephah. They shall be of fine flour, and they shall be baked with leaven, as firstfruits to the LORD.

**18** And you shall present with the bread seven lambs a year old without blemish, and one bull from the herd and two rams. They shall be a burnt offering to the LORD, with their grain offering and their drink offerings, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**19** And you shall offer one male goat for a sin offering, and two male lambs a year old as a sacrifice of peace offerings.

**20** And the priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits as a wave offering before the LORD, with the two lambs. They shall be holy to the LORD for the priest.

**21** And you shall make a proclamation on the same day. You shall hold a holy convocation. You shall do no ordinary work. It is a statute forever in all your dwelling places throughout your generations.

**22** "And when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right up to its edge, nor shall you gather the gleanings after your harvest. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner: I am the LORD your God."

**23** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**24** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: In the seventh month, on the first day of the month, you shall have a solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast, a holy convocation.

**25** You shall do no ordinary work, and you shall present a food offering to the LORD."

**26** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**27** "Now on the tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonement. It shall be for you a holy convocation, and you shall afflict yourselves and present a food offering to the LORD.

**28** And you shall do no work on that same day, for it is a Day of Atonement, to make atonement for you before the LORD your God.

**29** For whoever is not afflicted on that same day shall be cut off from his people.

**30** And whoever does any work on that same day, that person I will destroy from among his people.

**31** You shall do no work. It is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwelling places.

**32** It shall be to you a sabbath of solemn rest, and you shall afflict yourselves. On the ninth day of the month beginning at evening, from evening to evening, you shall keep your sabbath."

**33** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**34** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: On the fifteenth day of this seventh month and for seven days is the Feast of Booths to the LORD.

**35** On the first day shall be a holy convocation; you shall do no ordinary work.

**36** For seven days you shall present food offerings to the LORD. On the eighth day you shall hold a holy convocation and present a food offering to the LORD. It is a solemn assembly; you shall do no ordinary work.

**37** "These are the appointed times of the LORD, which you shall proclaim as holy convocations, for presenting to the LORD food offerings, burnt offerings and grain offerings, sacrifices and drink offerings, each on its proper day —

**38** besides the LORD's sabbaths and besides your gifts and besides all your vow offerings and besides all your freewill offerings, which you give to the LORD.

**39** "On the fifteenth day of the seventh month, when you have gathered in the produce of the land, you shall celebrate the feast of the LORD seven days. On the first day shall be a solemn rest, and on the eighth day shall be a solemn rest.

**40** And you shall take on the first day the fruit of splendid trees, branches of palm trees and boughs of leafy trees and willows of the brook, and you shall rejoice before the LORD your God seven days.

**41** You shall celebrate it as a feast to the LORD for seven days in the year. It is a statute forever throughout your generations; you shall celebrate it in the seventh month.

**42** You shall dwell in booths for seven days. All native Israelites shall dwell in booths,

**43** that your generations may know that I made the sons of Israel dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God."

**44** Thus Moses declared to the sons of Israel the appointed times of the LORD.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71v–72r:**
Leviticus 23 occupies folios 71v and 72r in the Leningrad Codex, representing the most expansive treatment of the sacred calendar in the Torah's priestly legislation. The chapter functions as the calendar-chapter of the Holiness Code (chs. 17–26), presenting the mo'adim — the divinely appointed times — as a temporal architecture structuring Israel's communal life with YHWH. Its 44 verses cover seven festivals (or eight, counting the eighth-day assembly of Tabernacles) in addition to the weekly Sabbath, making it the longest liturgical calendar text in the Hebrew Bible, though the parallel in Numbers 28–29 supplements it with more detailed sacrificial requirements.

The chapter's literary architecture is governed by a series of divine speech-introductions (vv. 1, 9, 23, 26, 33) that divide the material into five sections: (1) the programmatic introduction and the Sabbath (vv. 1–3); (2) the spring festivals — Passover, Unleavened Bread, and Firstfruits (vv. 4–22); (3) Trumpets (vv. 23–25); (4) Day of Atonement (vv. 26–32); and (5) Tabernacles (vv. 33–43), with a summary verse (v. 44). The chapter closes with a unique formula: "Thus Moses declared (vayyedabber Moshe) to the sons of Israel the appointed times of the LORD" — the only place in Leviticus where Moses is the subject of dibber rather than YHWH, marking the formal promulgation of the calendar to the community.

The Masoretic text in B19A is exceptionally well preserved in this chapter, with no major ketiv-qere variants affecting the substance of the translation. The chapter's formulaic repetition — miqra-qodesh ("holy convocation"), kol-melakhet avodah lo ta'asu ("you shall do no ordinary work"), isheh la-YHWH ("a food offering to the LORD") — reflects the structured, liturgical character of the priestly calendar legislation.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 109–110:**
The LXX of Leviticus 23 (pages 109–110, imaged as `LEV_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg` and `LEV_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`) is a generally faithful rendering of the MT, though with several notable divergences in festival vocabulary, count formulas, and the distinctive treatment of "the day after the sabbath" in v. 11. The LXX translators rendered mo'ed as καιρός (kairos, "appointed time, season") rather than ἑορτή (heorte, "feast"), which preserves the Hebrew emphasis on divine appointment. The term miqra-qodesh becomes ἐπίκλητος ἁγία (epikletos hagia, "a holy convocation/calling"), reflecting the sense of a called assembly.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "The appointed times of the LORD" (v. 2) — mo'adei YHWH:**
The noun mo'ed (מוֹעֵד, plural mo'adim) derives from the root ya'ad ("to appoint, to meet by appointment"). In priestly literature mo'ed carries a dual sense: (a) the appointed time itself — the calendar slot — and (b) the appointed meeting place, as in ohel mo'ed ("tent of meeting"). In Leviticus 23 the temporal sense predominates: these are YHWH's own appointments, times he has scheduled for encounter with his people. The rendering "appointed times" (following ESV/NASB) is superior to "feasts" (KJV) or "festivals" (NIV/NRSV), which lose the sense of divine scheduling and covenantal meeting. The LXX's καιροί (kairoi) — "appointed times, seasons" — supports this reading.

The possessive "of the LORD" (YHWH) is emphatic: these are not Israel's festivals to be kept at Israel's convenience but YHWH's own appointments, which Israel is commanded to proclaim (tiqre'u otam, "you shall proclaim/call them"). The use of qara' ("to call, to proclaim") governs both the festivals (miqra-qodesh, "a holy convocation/calling") and the calendar: Israel "calls" or "proclaims" the appointed times in the sense of formally declaring their arrival, which is why the verse says "which you shall proclaim as holy convocations."

**2. "Holy convocation" (throughout) — miqra qodesh:**
The phrase miqra qodesh (מִקְרָא קֹדֶשׁ) is the key liturgical technical term of Leviticus 23, occurring at each festival. Miqra derives from qara' ("to call, to proclaim, to summon") and denotes a "calling together," a "convocation" — a formal assembly of the community summoned by proclamation. Qodesh ("holy, set apart") qualifies the assembly as belonging to YHWH's sphere. The ESV/NASB rendering "holy convocation" (Latin: convocatio, "a calling together") is the most formally precise English equivalent, and is consistently used throughout this translation. Modern renderings such as "sacred assembly" (NIV) or "solemn assembly" (some older versions) are acceptable but less precise; "solemn assembly" in particular is reserved in this translation for atseret/atsarah, the eighth-day closing assembly of Tabernacles (v. 36).

**3. "Sabbath of solemn rest" (vv. 3, 32) — shabbat shabbaton:**
The doubled construction shabbat shabbaton (שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן) employs the intensive grammatical device of the cognate noun (the absolute state noun + the genitive of the same root) to denote the most complete, superlative quality of rest: "a sabbath of the most complete rest," "a sabbath of absolute cessation." The term shabbaton (as distinct from shabbat alone) appears also at the Day of Atonement (v. 32) and at the seventh-year sabbath of the land (25:4–5). The rendering "sabbath of solemn rest" (ESV) captures both the noun and the intensifier; "complete rest" (NASB) is also accurate. "Complete Sabbath" (some modern versions) is awkward. The LXX renders σάββατα ἀνάπαυσις (sabbata anapausis — "sabbath of repose"), using the noun anapausis ("cessation, rest") for the Hebrew shabbaton.

**4. "In the first month, on the fourteenth day... at twilight" (v. 5) — ben ha-arbayim:**
The phrase ben ha-arbayim (בֵּין הָעַרְבַּיִם, "between the two evenings") denotes the time of the Passover sacrifice. The precise delimitation has been debated since antiquity: the Pharisees and rabbis understood it as between sunset and nightfall (after the sun sets but while there is still light); the Samaritans and Karaites took it as between the afternoon decline of the sun and sunset (roughly 3–6 PM). The rendering "at twilight" (ESV) is the standard formal-equivalent translation; "between the evenings" (NASB margin) is more wooden but more literal. LOGOS uses "at twilight" as the most natural English equivalent that preserves the transitional-light sense without adjudicating the rabbinic debate.

**5. "Ordinary work" (throughout) — melakhet avodah:**
The phrase kol-melakhet avodah lo ta'asu ("you shall do no ordinary work") recurs at each festival day (vv. 7, 8, 21, 25, 35, 36). The construct melakhet avodah combines two Hebrew nouns for work/labor: melakhah (the standard term for work, the root of mal'akh — "messenger/agent" — connoting purposeful activity or craft) and avodah (service, labor, worship). Together they form a phrase that has been variously rendered "laborious work" (NASB), "regular work" (NIV), "occupational work" (some modern versions), "ordinary work" (ESV). The term distinguishes festival days from the weekly Sabbath: on the Sabbath, all melakhah whatsoever is forbidden (v. 3: kol-melakhah lo ta'asu); on festival days, the prohibition is narrowed to melakhet avodah — occupational or servile labor — while certain activities (cooking, carrying, etc.) may be permitted. The ESV's "ordinary work" is the most natural English rendering.

**6. "The day after the sabbath" (v. 11) — mi-macharat ha-shabbat:**
This phrase (מִמָּחֳרַת הַשַּׁבָּת) governs the timing of the omer wave offering and has generated one of the longest-running disputes in biblical interpretation and Jewish history. The question is whether ha-shabbat here means (a) the weekly seventh-day Sabbath (the view of the Sadducees, Boethusians, and later the Karaites), which would make the omer always on a Sunday, Firstfruits always on a Sunday, and Pentecost always on a Sunday — or (b) the first day of Unleavened Bread (15 Nisan), which is itself called a shabbat in the sense of a rest day (the Pharisaic/rabbinic view), making Pentecost always fall on the sixth of Sivan regardless of the day of the week. The Dead Sea community (4QMMT; Temple Scroll) followed interpretation (a). Rabbinic halakha established interpretation (b) definitively.

The LXX renders τῇ ἐπαύριον τῆς πρώτης ("on the day after the first [day]") — i.e., it interprets ha-shabbat as the first day of the feast (interpretation b), aligning with the Pharisaic reading. The LOGOS translation renders the Hebrew literally: "on the day after the sabbath," preserving the ambiguity of the text without adjudicating the interpretive tradition, in keeping with formal equivalence principles.

**7. "Seven complete weeks" (v. 15) — sheva shabbatot temimot:**
The counting formula sheva shabbatot temimot (שֶׁבַע שַׁבָּתוֹת תְּמִימֹת, "seven complete/whole weeks") uses shabbatot in the sense of "weeks" (each week being defined by its sabbath) and the adjective tamim (תָּמִים, "complete, whole, without blemish") to specify that the weeks must be full weeks — none may be cut short. The same term tamim is used of sacrificial animals "without blemish." The rendering "seven complete weeks" (ESV/NASB) is precise and traditional; "seven full weeks" is equally valid. The counting of fifty days from the omer to Shavuot is the origin of the Greek name "Pentecost" (pentekostos, "fiftieth").

**8. "Solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast" (v. 24) — zikhron teru'ah:**
The festival of the seventh month's first day is described by two terms in addition to "holy convocation" and "solemn rest": (1) zikhron (זִכְרוֹן, "memorial, remembrance, commemoration") and (2) teru'ah (תְּרוּעָה, "trumpet blast, war cry, shout"). The phrase zikhron teru'ah ("a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast") does not mention a shofar (ram's horn) explicitly, though Numbers 29:1 calls this day yom teru'ah ("a day of trumpet blast"). The teru'ah is a specific kind of blast — a staccato, broken-up pattern of notes (as opposed to the tekiah, a plain sustained blast) — used to summon the assembly, to signal war, and in sacred contexts to summon YHWH's attention to his people (Num 10:9–10). The rendering "a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast" (following ESV) preserves both the commemorative (zikhron) and the auditory (teru'ah) dimensions. The LXX renders μνημόσυνον σαλπίγγων ("a memorial of trumpets"), which becomes the origin of the Greek-Christian liturgical formula for this day.

The festival described here later becomes Rosh Hashanah (the Jewish New Year) in rabbinic tradition, though the text itself gives no indication that this is a new year observance — the "seventh month" is not the first month of the year in the Exodus calendar (that is Nisan, the first month, v. 5).

**9. "You shall afflict yourselves" (vv. 27, 29, 32) — ve-initem et-nafshoteikhem:**
The reflexive verb anah (עָנָה, Piel: "to afflict, to humble, to deny") applied to the nefesh ("self, soul, appetite") is the Torah's standard idiom for the bodily self-denial required on the Day of Atonement. Rabbinic interpretation identified five specific deprivations: (1) fasting from food and drink; (2) refraining from bathing; (3) refraining from anointing with oil; (4) refraining from wearing leather sandals; (5) refraining from conjugal relations. The phrase "afflict yourselves" (ESV/NASB/KJV) is the standard formal rendering, preserving the verb's sense of self-imposed suffering or humbling. Modern versions such as "deny yourselves" (NIV) or "practice self-denial" are accurate interpretively but lose the Hebrew's physical-suffering connotation. The LXX renders ταπεινώσετε τὰς ψυχὰς ὑμῶν ("you shall humble your souls"), using the verb tapeinoo ("to humble, to lower") — a semantically overlapping but not identical choice.

**10. "That person I will destroy" (v. 30) — ve-ha'abadti et ha-nefesh ha-hi:**
The verb in v. 30 shifts from the passive karet formula ("that person shall be cut off from his people," v. 29) to an emphatic active: ve-ha'abadti et ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-qerev ammah ("and I will destroy that person from the midst of his people"). The hiphil of abad ("to destroy, to cause to perish") with YHWH as the first-person subject is notably more direct than the karet formula and may denote a distinction between divine and communal enforcement: the karet of v. 29 may refer to communal excision while the ha'abadti of v. 30 refers to direct divine destruction. The LOGOS rendering "that person I will destroy" (following ESV/NASB) preserves the emphatic first-person construction with YHWH as agent.

**11. "Solemn assembly" (v. 36) — atseret:**
The term atseret (עֲצֶרֶת) from atsar ("to hold back, to restrain, to assemble") denotes a closing assembly — a "concluding convocation" — that marks the formal end of a festival period. It occurs at the eighth day of Tabernacles (here) and at the closing day of Passover/Unleavened Bread in Deuteronomy 16:8. The rendering "solemn assembly" (ESV/NASB/KJV) is traditional; "closing assembly" or "sacred assembly" are also defensible. The LOGOS translation uses "solemn assembly" to distinguish atseret from the regular miqra-qodesh ("holy convocation") formula used at the opening days of festivals.

**12. "The fruit of splendid trees" (v. 40) — pri ets hadar:**
The phrase pri ets hadar (פְּרִי עֵץ הָדָר, "fruit of splendid/beautiful trees") is the only description of what is to be taken on the first day of Tabernacles — it does not mention the etrog (citron) by name. Rabbinic tradition identified ets hadar as the etrog/citron, a ruling that became universally accepted in Judaism, making the etrog-lulav combination the defining ritual object of Sukkot. The term hadar (הָדָר) means "splendor, beauty, ornamentation" and can refer to the majestic appearance of the tree or the ornamental quality of its fruit. The rendering "the fruit of splendid trees" (following ESV's "fruit of splendid trees") preserves the literal breadth of the phrase without presupposing the rabbinic identification. The LXX renders καρπὸν ξύλου ὡραῖον ("fruit of a beautiful/seasonal tree"), using horaios ("beautiful, timely, in its season").

**13. "Boughs of leafy trees" (v. 40) — anaf ets avot:**
The phrase anaf ets avot (עֲנַף עֵץ עָבֹת, "branches of thick/leafy trees") refers to the myrtle in rabbinic interpretation (as part of the four-species lulav bundle). The adjective avot ("thick, dense, intertwined") describes trees with dense foliage. The LOGOS rendering "boughs of leafy trees" (following ESV) captures the sense without imposing the rabbinic identification.

**14. "You shall dwell in booths" (v. 42) — ba-sukkot teshvu shiv'at yamim:**
The verb yashav ("to sit, to dwell, to remain") in the context of ba-sukkot ("in booths") is the commandment that gives the Feast of Booths (Sukkot, from sukkah — a temporary shelter, booth, or tabernacle) its defining character. The commandment to actually dwell in (not merely build or observe) booths for seven days is applied specifically to kol ha-ezrach be-Yisra'el ("all native Israelites") — the ger (sojourner/resident alien) is not mentioned in this specific commandment, though sojourners are included in many Leviticus provisions. Verse 43 supplies the theological warrant: the booths memorialize the forty years of wilderness wandering when Israel dwelled in temporary shelters. The Hebrew sukkah can denote both (a) an agricultural harvest booth used by workers during the harvest season and (b) the wilderness shelters; the dual reference is part of the festival's layered significance.

---

### LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 109–110)

- **V. 2 — "appointed times" (mo'adim):** LXX renders as καιροί (kairoi, "appointed seasons"), whereas elsewhere in the LXX ἑορταί (heortai, "feasts") is used for the same word. The LXX of v. 2 introduces both καιροί and ἑορταί in the same verse — a conflation that may reflect the translator distinguishing the general appointed-time concept from the specific festivals.

- **V. 11 — "on the day after the sabbath":** LXX reads τῇ ἐπαύριον τῆς πρώτης ("on the day after the first [day]") rather than "on the day after the sabbath" — interpreting ha-shabbat as the first festival day (15 Nisan) rather than the weekly Sabbath. This is the Pharisaic/rabbinic interpretation. The MT's ha-shabbat is translated literally in LOGOS.

- **V. 13 — "a fourth of a hin":** LXX adds a specification about the type of wine (οἴνου τέταρτον τοῦ ῖν — "a quarter of the hin of wine") that corresponds to the MT but with slightly expanded phrasing.

- **V. 14 — "until you have brought the offering of your God":** The LXX renders "until this day you shall bring offerings to your God" — slightly restructuring the clause but without substantive difference.

- **V. 17 — "baked with leaven":** The LXX reads ἄρτους χοιμάσαντες ("prepared loaves"), possibly reflecting a slightly different Vorlage or an interpretive smoothing of the unusual requirement to present leavened loaves (chamets) — unusual because leaven is otherwise excluded from most altar offerings.

- **V. 22 — the gleaning-law:** The LXX renders this verse with slight expansions, adding "you shall not return to gather them" (reinforcing the prohibition on gleaning one's own field), corresponding to 19:9–10. This may be a harmonizing addition by the LXX translator.

- **V. 24 — "a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast" (zikhron teru'ah):** LXX: μνημόσυνον σαλπίγγων ("memorial of trumpets") — the plural σαλπίγγων ("of trumpets") may reflect a different reading or simply the LXX's expansion for clarity.

- **V. 36 — "solemn assembly" (atseret):** LXX renders ἐξόδιον ("closing/departure [feast]"), from exodos — a striking choice that emphasizes the concluding character of the assembly as a formal "departure" from the festival season.

- **V. 40 — "fruit of splendid trees" (pri ets hadar):** LXX: καρπὸν ξύλου ὡραῖον ("fruit of a beautiful/timely tree") — horaios can mean "beautiful" or "in its prime season." Neither the MT nor the LXX specifies the etrog by name.

- **V. 43 — "when I brought them out of the land of Egypt":** The LXX corresponds closely to the MT here, preserving the Exodus memorial formula that underlies the Feast of Booths' theological rationale.

---

### Summary

Leviticus 23 is the sacred calendar of ancient Israel presented as YHWH's own appointed times — his scheduled appointments for encounter with his people. The chapter moves from the weekly Sabbath (v. 3), through the spring festivals rooted in the Exodus and the grain harvest (Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Weeks), to the autumn festivals of the seventh month (Trumpets, Day of Atonement, Tabernacles), creating a temporal liturgical framework that structures the entire Israelite year around the acts of YHWH — the Exodus, the wilderness provision, the harvest bounty, and the need for atonement.

The LOGOS translation consistently follows ESV/NASB formal equivalence priorities: preserving the technical vocabulary (mo'ed, miqra-qodesh, shabbat shabbaton, teru'ah, atseret) with well-established English equivalents, maintaining literal syntax where possible, and supplying notes on the historically contested interpretive questions (the omer-day debate, the etrog identification, the nature of teru'ah) without adjudicating them in the translation itself. The gleaning law of v. 22 — embedded within the festival calendar — is a characteristic Holiness Code move: the agricultural care for the vulnerable is not incidental but belongs to the same covenantal framework as the sacred feasts. The chapter's final verse, with Moses as the declared subject, marks the formal moment of liturgical constitution: Israel's calendar is not a human cultural construct but a divinely appointed, Mosaic-promulgated ordering of time itself.
